feedback.text: My heartfelt greetings to anyone reading this. Before my longer text, I shall simply state my request. That the Stop Killing Games movement and the Stop Destroying Videogames EU Citizens Initiative also be considered or "tackled" in this initiative. I shall also preface this by apologizing for not being very well versed in all the legal jargon. I may have incorrectly used some terms but my argument still stands. Now onto what I wish to say as a consumer. For decades now has the Video Games industry been largely ignored by the rest of the world. Between things having been and still being illegal in real life while legal in-game, such as gambling through systems like "lootboxes" or "gacha" as a minor, and things such as extremely aggressive marketing and pushing of predatory micro-transactions towards minors which, while maybe not illegal, can still easily be described as morally questionable. This brings me to another issue these monopolies of the video games industry have created. Most video-games nowadays are sold as a "service", meaning you do not purchase the game, you purchase a license to play said game which can be revoked at any time, and under many EULAs, for any reason which under some EULAs, needn't even be disclosed (see Blizzard and Ubisoft for example). While many gamers vaguely understand this, it is not made nearly clear enough that this is the case, but more importantly: I firmly believe this SHOULD NOT be the case. Picture the following: You go to the dealership, and buy a brand new car. You're happy with your car, yet 5 years later or so the manufacturer announces they will no manufacture that model nor it's specific parts. This means you may need to buy used car pieces if you would need to fix it, or create them yourself if you have a talent for engineering. But imagine that when this is announced, the manufacturer would send you an email saying they will take the car back in 6 months. Then 6 months later, without anything you can do about it, the car is simply taken away. Of course there is the blaring difference between cars and video games not being nearly in the same price-range, but the principle still stands: I want an end of life act for videogames-- even online-only ones. This could be making the game available if hosted and played locally, or perhaps releasing the code to host a server for the game yourself. This would be the equivalent of making your own car parts. While some companies have claimed this would "curtail developer's choice" (or something similar, I may be paraphrasing), that is-- respectfully-- a load of bologna. Any new laws would technically curtail developer choice and making the production of games more expensive. But most game developers that speak on this say it would be remarkably easy and cheap. Many believe the real reason companies don't wish to do this, is to push consumers to buy newer video games from them. Such as with "The Crew", a game that recently went offline and is now no longer playable whatsoever. This game however has received sequels, one of which was very recent (within the last year). So people who liked "The Crew" and want to keep playing it, are now be forced to buy said newer game to do so. To summarize: I believe the video games industry is very pervasive and predatory, the most prevalent and media-present issue of which currently is it's ability to completely and utterly revoke consumers' access to things that they have purchased. The fix to this would not cost game companies much more money to make, it would only cut some revenue in protection of the customers. So let's help the little man in our wonderful union, and see that this issue gets additional attention in this initiative. Thank you for reading this, I hope I was able to convey my standpoint effeciently.
